title: CSS Foundations
tagline: Make things colorful, sized, and positioned.

accomplishments: Change the background color of a webpage

do.title: Make your webpage turn blue
steps: Open VS Code (or use CodePen.io if you don't have VS Code installed yet)
Create a new file and save it as colors.html
Copy and paste the code below
Double-click the file to open it in your browser
Your whole page should have a light blue background!
do.code:
Look! A blue background!

This is my first styled webpage.
do.result: You just used CSS to change the background color of your entire webpage!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing your light blue webpage.

concepts.name: The <style> tag
concepts.definition: This is where CSS code lives inside an HTML file. Everything between <style> and </style> is CSS, not HTML.
concepts.analogy: A painter's instructions before they start painting. The <style> tag says "Here's how I want things to look."

concepts.name: The body selector
concepts.definition: This tells CSS "I want to style the body element." The body is the entire visible part of your webpage — everything you can see.
concepts.analogy: Pointing at a room and saying "paint THIS room blue." The selector picks what to paint.

concepts.name: The background-color property
concepts.definition: This is the instruction that actually changes the color. You write the property name, then a colon, then the color you want, then a semicolon.
concepts.analogy: A recipe instruction: ingredient (selector), cooking method (property), specific measurement (value). body { background-color: lightblue; }

understand.keyTakeaway: CSS changes how things LOOK. HTML creates the things, CSS makes them pretty.

build.goal: Make the heading red while keeping the blue background

codeComparison.new:
<style>
  body {
    background-color: lightblue;
  }
  
  h1 {
    color: red;
  }
</style>

build.hint: Make sure you have both closing curly braces }. One closes the body rule, one closes the h1 rule.

challenge.title: Create a Traffic Light
challenge.mission: Create three headings that look like a traffic light — one red, one yellow, one green.
requirements: "Slow" is yellow (or gold — yellow is hard to read!)
challenge.starterCode:
Stop

Slow

Go
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add font-size: 60px; to make them HUGE like real traffic lights!

capabilities: Add CSS to an HTML file using the <style> tag
Target specific HTML elements with CSS selectors
Use classes to style different elements differently

bridge.preview: In Lesson 1.2, you'll learn to control text size, weight, and font families. You'll make headlines that actually look like headlines!
